This `else` block makes the deserialization strict by throwing on unknown properties. However, combined with the `return changeArgs;` on line 37, the behavior becomes dependent on the order of properties in the JSON object. If an unknown property appears before the `value` property, this will throw an exception. If it appears after, it will be silently ignored due to the early return.

Since JSON property order is not guaranteed, this can lead to unpredictable behavior.

To implement a consistently strict policy, please remove the `return changeArgs;` from inside the `if` block. This will ensure all properties are iterated over and validated. Most of the other new readers in this PR (e.g., `ErrorEventArgsReader`, `KeyboardEventArgsReader`) correctly follow this pattern.

Alternatively, if the goal is to be lenient and ignore unknown properties (which is generally more robust for versioning), this `else` block should be removed entirely.

For better diagnostics, it would be helpful to include the type of object being deserialized in the exception message. This can make it easier to debug issues with JSON payloads, especially when multiple types are being processed.

For example:
```csharp
throw new JsonException($"Unknown property '{property.Name}' found when deserializing '{nameof(ClipboardEventArgs)}'.");
```

This same suggestion applies to the other `EventArgs` readers being added in this PR for consistency.

Excellent optimization! Consolidating the property processing into a single loop avoids iterating over the `JsonElement`'s properties twice (once in the now-removed `MouseEventArgsReader.Read` call and again in the loop that followed). This single-pass approach is a great performance improvement for this hot path.

This change makes the JSON parsing strict by throwing an exception for any unknown properties. This is a deviation from the default behavior of `System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer`, which is to ignore unknown members for forward compatibility.

While this helps in catching unexpected data, it can also make the application brittle. For example, if a future version of the Blazor JavaScript runtime adds a new property to the event data, older applications would break upon receiving it.

Was this strictness intentional? A more resilient approach would be to simply ignore unknown properties in the `else` block, which would align with the robustness principle of being liberal in what you accept.

Throwing an exception for an unknown property makes the deserialization logic brittle and can cause forward compatibility issues. If a future version of the Blazor client-side script adds a new property to the event data payload for any reason, older applications using this logic would break upon receiving it.

It's generally safer and more robust to ignore unknown properties, which is also the default behavior of `System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer`. Consider removing this `else` block to simply ignore any properties that are not recognized.

Throwing an exception for an unknown property could make applications more brittle. Browser event payloads can change over time (e.g., new properties being added in new browser versions). If a browser adds a new property to the keyboard event, this would cause Blazor applications to throw an exception and fail to handle the event.

It's generally safer to ignore unknown properties to ensure forward compatibility. This is also the default behavior of `JsonSerializer`.

Consider removing this `else` block to silently ignore any unrecognized properties.

Throwing an exception for an unknown property makes this deserialization logic brittle and breaks forward compatibility. If a future version of the Blazor client-side code (or the browser itself) adds new properties to the mouse event payload, this will cause older applications to throw exceptions and break.

It's generally better to be tolerant of unexpected data in payloads like this. I recommend removing this `else` block to simply ignore any unknown properties, which is the default behavior for `System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer` and most other JSON libraries.

This new approach of delegating to `MouseEventArgsReader.ReadProperty` for properties not handled here is a great optimization. By processing all properties in a single loop instead of iterating over the `JsonElement` twice (once for `MouseEventArgs` properties and once for `PointerEventArgs` properties), the deserialization becomes more efficient.

Throwing an exception for an unknown property makes this JSON deserialization logic brittle. Event payloads from the browser can change over time (e.g., new properties added to browser specifications, or browser-specific extensions). It's generally safer to ignore unknown properties to ensure forward compatibility. This would also align with the default behavior of `System.Text.Json.JsonSerializer`.

I'd recommend removing this `else` block to silently ignore any properties that are not explicitly handled. This same feedback likely applies to the other new event argument readers in this pull request.

Throwing an exception for unknown properties makes this deserialization contract very strict and could cause forward compatibility problems. If a future version of the Blazor client-side scripts adds a new property to this event payload, it will break older applications that haven't been updated.

The `JsonSerializer` this code replaces ignores unknown properties by default, which is generally safer for this kind of client-server communication.

Was this stricter behavior an intentional choice? If not, I'd suggest removing this `else` block to simply ignore unknown properties. This same comment applies to the similar change on line 72.

Changing the series of `if` statements to an `if-else if` chain is a good improvement. It's slightly more performant as it avoids unnecessary checks once a property is matched. More importantly, combined with the new `else` block that throws for unknown properties, it makes the parser stricter. This is a good "fail-fast" approach that helps ensure payload correctness and can surface issues with mismatched data contracts early.

This new approach of delegating to a `ReadProperty` method on the base class reader is a great way to handle deserialization for derived types. It correctly avoids iterating over the JSON properties multiple times (once for the base type, and again for the derived type), which was a performance issue in the previous implementation. This change improves efficiency by processing each property only once.

Good change. Using the `!=` operator for enum comparison is slightly more performant as it avoids the boxing that would occur with `_fields.Equals(...)`. This correctly implements the check for changes in logging fields, addressing the issue of creating a new file when the configuration is updated.

This test correctly verifies that changing `LoggingFields` and notifying the options monitor causes a new log file to be created. This is a great test for the reported issue.

To make the test suite even more robust, consider also verifying the negative case: an options change that does *not* modify `LoggingFields` should *not* trigger a file roll.

For example, you could change a different property like `FileSizeLimit`, invoke the change, enqueue another message, and assert that it's written to the *same* file before you then change the `LoggingFields`. This would ensure the file roll logic is tied specifically to `LoggingFields` changing, as described in the issue.

This will throw a `NullReferenceException` if `OnChange` was never called, as `_listener` would be `null`. You should use the null-conditional operator to safely invoke the listener: `_listener?.Invoke(CurrentValue, null);`.

Also, a related point on `OnChange`: it currently uses assignment (`_listener = listener;`), which means it only supports a single listener. If `OnChange` is called multiple times, only the last listener will be registered. While this might be sufficient for current tests, the standard pattern for `IOptionsMonitor` is to support multiple listeners. You might consider changing the assignment to `_listener += listener;` to align with this pattern and make the test helper more robust.

The issue description and proposed API changes specify a new property `IsRequired` on `ConsumesAttribute`. However, this test is using `IsOptional`. To align with the OpenAPI specification and the agreed-upon API design, please use `IsRequired`. This would mean changing `IsOptional = true` to `IsRequired = false`.

The same applies to the other test modification in this file (line 113) where `IsOptional = false` should be `IsRequired = true`.

The `AddsJsonRequestFormatWhenFromBodyInferred` test was removed in this change. This test seems to cover an important default behavior for inferring the content type for parameters bound from the body. Could you clarify if this behavior is now tested elsewhere? It's important to ensure we don't have a regression in the default inference logic.

The test name is a bit unconventional as it describes a negative outcome. A name following the `UnitOfWork_StateUnderTest_ExpectedBehavior` convention, like `GetApiDescription_WithBodyParameter_DoesNotCreateParameterDescription`, might be more aligned with common testing practices.

More importantly, this test (and others in this file) now only verifies that a body parameter is no longer added to `ApiDescription.ParameterDescriptions`. This is a significant behavioral change, presumably because the request body is now being described via `ApiDescription.RequestBody`. However, there are no new assertions to verify that `ApiDescription.RequestBody` is correctly populated. Please add tests to cover the new behavior and ensure the request body metadata (like type, content types, and whether it's required) is correctly set on `ApiDescription.RequestBody`.

As a minor point, the helper method `AssertBodyParameter` is now misleadingly named and its `expectedType` parameter is unused, so it should be refactored or removed.
Good catch. Adding a period here improves documentation consistency and aligns with standard C# XML comment guidelines.

The `entityTag` parameter should probably be nullable (`EntityTagHeaderValue?`) to maintain consistency with other `File` overloads in `ControllerBase`. For example, `File(Stream, ...)` and `PhysicalFile(...)` both accept a nullable `EntityTagHeaderValue?`. Making this non-nullable would force consumers to always provide an ETag, which might not be intended.

This also applies to the overload on line 1760.

This registration for the new `PushFileStreamResultExecutor` correctly follows the established pattern for action result executors in MVC. Using `TryAddSingleton` is consistent with the other executors and is the correct lifetime, as these executors are typically stateless. This is a key step in enabling the new push-based stream result type.

For consistency with other `FileResult` executors like `FileStreamResultExecutor` and `FileContentResultExecutor`, could you please rename this method to `ExecutingFileResult`?

Those executors also use a logger method with `EventName = "ExecutingFileResultWithNoFileName"`, but the C# method itself is named `ExecutingFileResult`. While your naming (`ExecutingFileResultWithNoFileName`) is arguably more consistent with the event name, it's preferable to follow the existing pattern in the codebase.

This would also require updating the call at line 77.

The `entityTag` parameter is defined as non-nullable (`EntityTagHeaderValue!`), which seems incorrect. For consistency with other `File` overloads on `ControllerBase` and the related `Results.Stream` API proposed in the issue, this parameter should be nullable (`EntityTagHeaderValue?`). An ETag is an optional header for caching, so requiring it would limit the usefulness of this method. This also applies to the overload on the next line.

For consistency with other `FileResult` types, the property that provides the content should be immutable after construction. For example, `FileStreamResult.FileStream` and `FileContentResult.FileContents` are get-only properties.

Since `StreamWriterCallback` is the content provider for this result, it should also be read-only. The value is set in the constructor and isn't expected to change.

Please consider changing this to a get-only property:
```csharp
public Func<Stream, Task> StreamWriterCallback { get; }
```

This local variable `expectedMediaType` is redundant. It's assigned the value of `contentType` and then used once in the assertion on line 50. You can remove this variable and use `contentType` directly in the assertion to make the code more concise. A similar redundant variable exists in the `Constructor_SetsLastModifiedAndEtag` test on line 59.

There's a small inconsistency in how byte arrays are created from strings in these tests. Here you're using `"..."u8.ToArray()`, while the `WriteFileAsync_RangeProcessingNotEnabled_RangeRequestedIgnored` test uses `Encoding.ASCII.GetBytes`. For consistency across the test file, I'd suggest picking one style. `Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(...)` is a good, explicit, and common choice, and UTF-8 is generally preferred over ASCII.

This change to the signature of `ConfigureWebDefaultsCore` removes the `configureRouting` parameter. As a result, callers like `WebApplication.CreateSlimBuilder` (which I assume calls this) will no longer have routing services registered by default.

While this aligns with the 'pay-for-play' goal for the slim builder, it's a significant change beyond the scope of TLS and QUIC mentioned in the issue and PR description.

Could you please confirm this is intentional? If so, it would be beneficial to update the PR description to mention that routing is also not included in the slim builder by default, to avoid surprising developers.

This logic for setting `ASPNETCORE_URLS` is duplicated from `StartBuiltProjectAsync` (line 189). The environment dictionaries in both methods are very similar. To avoid duplication, consider extracting the dictionary creation into a private helper method. This helper could accept parameters for `noHttps` and whether it's a development environment.

This change to pass `noHttps: true` seems correct given that the PR makes TLS support opt-in for the API template. 

However, the preceding comment on line 85 (`// The minimal/slim/core scenario...`) could be a bit misleading. It suggests this logic is specific to a 'slim' scenario, but the change is applied unconditionally to all tests using the `ApiTemplateCore` helper. 

To improve clarity, could you update the comment to reflect that this is the new default behavior for the API template? For example: 
`// The default API template now uses a slim configuration that doesn't include TLS support by default, so we must prevent the test harness from trying to use HTTPS.`

The phrase "this functionality" is a bit vague. Given that this error will appear in new scenarios for users opting into the "slim" builder, it would be more helpful to be explicit about what functionality is missing and why it needs to be enabled.

Consider a more descriptive message, for example: "HTTPS support is not enabled by default. Call UseHttpsConfiguration() on IWebHostBuilder to enable it."

This clarifies what is missing (HTTPS support) and that it requires explicit action, which should help developers who encounter this new behavior understand the problem and solution more quickly.

For improved readability and to make it explicit that the lambda parameters are intentionally unused, consider using discards (`_`).

```csharp
OnConnection = (_, _) => ValueTask.FromResult(sslServerAuthenticationOptions),
```
There's a small typo here. I think you mean 'but will only be' instead of 'by only be'.
Typo: 'off' should be 'of'.

This is a great application of the strategy pattern using a delegate. Replacing the direct call to `options.UseHttps()` with `_useHttps(options)` effectively decouples the address binding logic from the HTTPS configuration. This is a key change for enabling the "pay-for-play" TLS feature, as it allows the caller to control the HTTPS configuration behavior (e.g., to throw a helpful error when TLS is not enabled). The implementation is clean and fits the overall goal of the PR well.

This is an excellent refactoring. Delegating the HTTPS configuration for multiplexed transports to the `IHttpsConfigurationService` significantly cleans up the `TransportManager` and improves separation of concerns.

This change is key to the "pay-for-play" goal, as it moves complex, HTTPS-specific logic (including the `ConnectionContextAdapter` that was previously here) out of the core transport management path. This makes the code more modular and is what allows the trimmer to remove HTTPS-related code when it's not used. Great work!

This updated error message is a great improvement. It provides clear and actionable guidance to developers, especially those using the new 'slim' builder who might be surprised that QUIC isn't enabled by default. It correctly points them towards `UseQuic` as a solution, which is a key part of the 'pay-for-play' user experience.

This is a nice refactoring. Encapsulating the logic for checking the presence of a server certificate or a selector into the `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` property makes this code cleaner and more expressive. It improves readability and centralizes the logic where it belongs, in `HttpsConnectionAdapterOptions`.

The `!listenOptions.IsTls` check was removed from this condition. This seems to be a regression.

The preceding comment `// EndpointDefaults or configureEndpoint may have added an https adapter.` indicates that HTTPS might have already been configured for this endpoint in code (e.g., via `options.Listen(..., o => o.UseHttps())`).

Previously, if `listenOptions.IsTls` was true, this block would be skipped, effectively letting code-based configuration take precedence over configuration from `appsettings.json`.

With this change, `_httpsConfigurationService.UseHttpsWithSni` will be called even if HTTPS is already configured. This will likely lead to an `InvalidOperationException` because `UseHttps` would be called a second time on the same `ListenOptions`.

Was this behavior change intentional? If not, please consider re-adding the `!listenOptions.IsTls` check to preserve the old behavior and prevent crashes when HTTPS is configured both in code and configuration files.

Instead of throwing `NotImplementedException`, it would be more helpful to developers to throw a `NotSupportedException` with a descriptive message. This aligns with the PR's goal of providing clear guidance when a feature is used in an unsupported configuration.

For example:
```csharp
throw new NotSupportedException(
    "Configuring HTTPS endpoints from IConfiguration is not supported when KestrelServer is constructed with this overload. " +
    "To use IConfiguration for HTTPS, use WebApplication.CreateBuilder or Host.CreateDefaultBuilder and configure Kestrel through the web host builder.");
```

This change should also be applied to the `UseHttpsWithSni` and `LoadDefaultCertificate` methods in this class, which also currently throw `NotImplementedException`.

This is a nice refactoring. Encapsulating the logic for checking if a server certificate is configured into the `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` property improves readability and makes the `HttpsConnectionAdapterOptions` class more responsible for its own state.

This is a good refactoring. Using the `HasServerCertificateOrSelector` property improves readability and encapsulates the logic for checking certificate presence within the `HttpsConnectionAdapterOptions` class. This makes the code cleaner and more maintainable.

Since this delegate is stateless, consider making it `static readonly`. This will prevent a new delegate instance from being created for each test class instance. Xunit creates a new instance of the test class for each test method, so this is a small optimization.

You could also adopt the `s_` prefix convention for static fields, which is common in this repository:
`private static readonly Func<ListenOptions, ListenOptions> s_noopUseHttps = l => l;`

With the introduction of `IHttpsConfigurationService` as a required service that's resolved during `KestrelServerOptions.Configure()`, adding a mock for it here is necessary for this test to pass. This seems correct, as this particular test isn't concerned with HTTPS functionality but rather the overall configuration and listen flow. Using `Mock.Of<T>()` is sufficient since the service's methods won't be invoked for a non-HTTPS endpoint.

I've noticed that the new `IHttpsConfigurationService` dependency is being provided in several different ways across the tests in this file:
1. As an uninitialized `new HttpsConfigurationService()` here.
2. As an initialized instance in the `CreateKestrelServer` helper.
3. As a mock with `IsInitialized` returning `true` in `CreateServerOptions`.
4. As a default mock (`Mock.Of<IHttpsConfigurationService>()`) in other tests.

This inconsistency can make the tests brittle and harder to maintain. While an uninitialized service works for this specific test, it might cause subtle failures if the test is modified in the future to include HTTPS endpoints.

Could we standardize the approach? For tests like this that don't rely on HTTPS functionality, using a mock (perhaps from a shared helper) as done in `CreateServerOptions` seems like a more robust and consistent pattern.

As you've noted in the PR description, the name `UseKestrelSlim` is TBD. While 'Slim' is concise, it can be a bit ambiguous about what is excluded. Have you considered `UseKestrelCore`? It might better convey that this is a foundational set of Kestrel features that can be extended with other calls like `UseHttpsConfiguration` or `UseQuic`. The 'Core' suffix is often used in .NET to denote a minimal base, which seems to fit the goal here.

Using `AddSingleton` here means that if `UseHttpsConfiguration()` is called multiple times, it will register the `IInitializer` service multiple times. While this might be harmless depending on how `IInitializer` is consumed (e.g., if it's resolved as `IEnumerable<IInitializer>` and only a presence check is done), it's generally better to use `TryAddSingleton` for this kind of "enabler" pattern.

Using `TryAddSingleton` would make this method idempotent, which aligns perfectly with the XML comment on line 28: "Has no effect in `UseKestrel(IWebHostBuilder)` scenarios." With `TryAddSingleton`, subsequent calls would truly have no effect on the service container.

Consider changing this to:
`services.TryAddSingleton<HttpsConfigurationService.IInitializer, HttpsConfigurationService.Initializer>();`

The comment on the preceding line, `// This is more documentary than normative`, somewhat undermines the value of this assertion. This test appears to be a crucial validation of the 'pay-for-play' behavior for HTTPS. When a certificate is provided directly, the global HTTPS configuration services shouldn't be initialized, which is what this assertion correctly checks to ensure dependencies aren't unnecessarily pulled in.

Could you consider rephrasing the comment to better explain the intent and affirm its importance? For example:

```csharp
// Verify that when a certificate is provided directly to UseHttps, the global HttpsConfigurationService is not initialized.
// This is an important part of the pay-for-play feature to ensure unused services aren't activated.
```

This would make the purpose of the test clearer and more assertive.

I've noticed that the registration of `IHttpsConfigurationService` and `HttpsConfigurationService.IInitializer` is repeated in several test files in this pull request. To improve maintainability and reduce code duplication, consider introducing a shared extension method on `IServiceCollection`.

For instance, a method like `AddKestrelHttpsConfiguration()` in a test utility class could encapsulate these two registrations. This would make the test setup cleaner and more consistent across the different test projects where this is needed.

While you're modifying this line, consider resolving `IMultiplexedConnectionListenerFactory` from the service provider instead of hardcoding `Array.Empty`. Using `sp.GetServices<IMultiplexedConnectionListenerFactory>()` would be more consistent with how `IConnectionListenerFactory` is resolved.

This would make the `TestServer` more flexible, allowing tests to opt into QUIC testing by registering an `IMultiplexedConnectionListenerFactory`. In the default case where none is registered, `GetServices` returns an empty enumerable, preserving the current behavior of disabling QUIC.

The PR description mentions that there might be a better way to handle feature enablement via DI. This "marker service" pattern of registering `IInitializer` to enable HTTPS functionality is indeed a bit implicit and might be hard for users to discover.

A more common pattern in ASP.NET Core is to use `IOptions`. For example:
1. Introduce an `HttpsOptions` class with an `Enabled` property.
2. `UseHttpsConfiguration` would call `services.Configure<HttpsOptions>(o => o.Enabled = true)`.
3. `HttpsConfigurationService` would inject `IOptions<HttpsOptions>` and check the flag.

This approach is more explicit and aligns with existing conventions for configuring features.

If you decide to stick with the current approach, consider renaming `IInitializer` to something that better reflects its role as an enabler, like `HttpsFeatureEnabler`, since its presence seems to be the key.

For consistency and clarity, could you add a comment explaining why `IHostEnvironment` is needed here? Similar to the comment for `IHttpsConfigurationService`, it would help future maintainers understand the purpose of this mock. For example:

```csharp
// The default certificate loading logic has a dependency on IHostEnvironment.
.AddSingleton(Mock.Of<IHostEnvironment>())
```

I see you're using an `IInitializer` service to enable `HttpsConfigurationService`, and noted in the PR description that you were looking for other ways to do this in DI.

An alternative pattern you could consider is service replacement. This might be slightly cleaner as it avoids the nested `IInitializer` type and the `IEnumerable<IInitializer>` dependency.

It would look something like this:
1.  Define a `DisabledHttpsConfigurationService` that implements `IHttpsConfigurationService` by throwing.
2.  In the default/slim Kestrel setup, register it: `services.TryAddSingleton<IHttpsConfigurationService, DisabledHttpsConfigurationService>()`.
3.  The `UseHttpsConfiguration()` extension method would then register the full implementation: `services.AddSingleton<IHttpsConfigurationService, HttpsConfigurationService>()`.

Because `TryAdd` is used, if `UseHttpsConfiguration()` is called, its registration of the full service will be used. If not, the disabled service will be used. This pattern is also very friendly to the trimmer, as the full `HttpsConfigurationService` and its dependencies wouldn't be rooted if it's not used.

The current approach is also valid, but this alternative might be worth considering for its relative simplicity.

The key name `".refreshToken"` is quite generic. While this is consistent with other keys in this class (e.g., `".redirect"`, `".refresh"`), it could lead to potential conflicts if multiple authentication handlers that use refresh tokens are active in the same application and both attempt to use this property.

Have you considered a more specific key, for example `".bearer.refreshToken"`, to namespace it for the bearer token handler? This would make it more robust against future additions to the framework or interactions with third-party handlers. The public property can, of course, remain named `RefreshToken`.

While adding a `RefreshToken` property is necessary for this feature, adding it directly to the general-purpose `AuthenticationProperties` class might not be the ideal design. This class is shared across all authentication schemes, many of which don't use refresh tokens.

As mentioned in the 'Alternative Designs' section of the issue, have you considered creating a more specific derived class, like `BearerTokenProperties`? This would follow the established pattern of `OAuthChallengeProperties` and keep the base `AuthenticationProperties` class cleaner.

If the decision is to keep it on `AuthenticationProperties`, please ensure it's implemented as a wrapper around the `Items` dictionary with a corresponding public static key (e.g., `public static readonly string RefreshTokenKey`). This is critical for serialization and is consistent with how other properties on this class are implemented.

To make the JSON contract explicit, consider adding the `[JsonPropertyName]` attribute.

The client usage example in the issue description (`PostAsJsonAsync(..., new { refreshToken })`) will produce a JSON property named `refreshToken` (camelCase). While the default deserializer is case-insensitive, explicitly specifying the name with `[JsonPropertyName("refreshToken")]` makes the contract clearer and more robust against potential changes in serialization settings.

This also helps resolve ambiguity, as the issue description also contains a comment suggesting an all-lowercase `refreshtoken` property, while the example response uses snake_case `refresh_token`.

The comment "The sign in will fail without an identity name" seems confusing and potentially incorrect in the context of a token refresh. Based on the proposed design, the `BearerTokenHandler`'s refresh logic relies on the `RefreshToken` from `AuthenticationProperties` to reconstruct the principal and should ignore the placeholder principal passed to `SignIn`. The placeholder principal's name should not matter.

Please consider revising the comment for clarity. For example:
"// This minimal principal is used to trigger the sign-in flow. The BearerTokenHandler will then use the refresh token from the properties to reconstruct the full principal."

The XML comments for this method and its overload on line 25 appear to be copy-pasted from a cookie authentication method. They should be updated to accurately describe that they configure bearer token authentication for Identity.
There's a typo here. "ans" should be "and".

The issue description highlights the new `AddIdentityBearerToken<TUser>` method as the preferred way to integrate bearer token authentication with Identity, as it wires up security stamp validation. To guide users correctly, should this documentation point to `IdentityAuthenticationBuilderExtensions.AddIdentityBearerToken` instead of the generic `BearerTokenExtensions.AddBearerToken`? This would also be more consistent with the reference to `AddIdentityCookies` on the next line, which is also Identity-specific.

The `CompositeIdentityHandler` is only intended to handle authentication by composing bearer and cookie schemes, while sign-in and sign-out operations are forwarded. Inheriting from `SignInAuthenticationHandler` requires implementing `HandleSignInAsync` and `HandleSignOutAsync`, which currently throw `NotImplementedException`.

A cleaner approach would be to inherit from `AuthenticationHandler<AuthenticationSchemeOptions>`. This base class only requires implementing `HandleAuthenticateAsync`. The base implementations of `HandleSignInAsync` and `HandleSignOutAsync` already throw `NotSupportedException`, which is more semantically correct if they were ever called due to a misconfiguration. This would simplify `CompositeIdentityHandler` by removing the need for the `HandleSignInAsync` and `HandleSignOutAsync` overrides, making the handler's purpose clearer and more robust.

The parameter name `configureOptions` is inconsistent with the `AddIdentityApiEndpoints` method (see line 14), which uses `configure`. For consistency across the Identity API endpoints feature, please consider renaming this parameter to `configure`. This also aligns with the proposed API in the issue description.

This change adds the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Test` project to the `Identity.slnf` solution filter. While this seems plausible for testing the new refresh token integration, could you please confirm this is necessary and briefly explain the dependency? Solution filters are meant to keep developer focus narrow, so we should be careful about adding broader dependencies like a general test project unless required.

This test is named `CanUseRefreshToken`, and it does call the refresh endpoint. However, it then uses the `access_token` from the original login response (`loginContent`) rather than the new one from the refresh response (`refreshContent`). To properly test that the refresh was successful and produced a valid new token, you should be using the `access_token` from `refreshContent`.

This appears to be a copy-paste error. The `accessToken` variable is being assigned the value of `refresh_token`. It should be assigned the `access_token` from the `loginContent`. While the test might pass by coincidence (as using a refresh token as a bearer token will result in a 401), it's not testing the intended scenario of using an expired access token.

Renaming `MessageReceived` to `MessageReceivedAsync` is a good change. It aligns the method name with the standard .NET async naming conventions for methods that return a `Task`, improving consistency within the ASP.NET Core framework.

The authentication scheme for the refresh ticket is being set to a composite string `{Scheme.Name}:{RefreshTokenPurpose}`. This is unconventional and could be confusing, as the `AuthenticationScheme` property on an `AuthenticationTicket` is expected to correspond to a registered authentication scheme name.

The distinction between the access token and the refresh token is already correctly handled by using different `purpose` strings (`BearerTokenPurpose` and `RefreshTokenPurpose`) when calling `TokenProtector.Protect`/`Unprotect`. This is a good use of the data protection API to create distinct security contexts for the two types of tokens.

Using a modified scheme name for the refresh ticket is redundant and could cause issues if other parts of the system (or custom implementations of `ISecureDataFormat`) inspect the ticket's `AuthenticationScheme` property and expect a valid, registered scheme name.

To keep things simple and conventional, I suggest using `Scheme.Name` for the refresh ticket's authentication scheme, just like it's done for the access ticket in `CreateAccessTicket`.

The rename from `BearerTokenProtector` is a good improvement for accuracy now that it handles refresh tokens. However, `TokenProtector` is a bit generic and could be interpreted as protecting a single token type. Since this protects the data for both bearer and refresh tokens, would `TokensProtector` (plural) be a more accurate name? This is a minor suggestion, but could improve clarity.

For better traceability and auditing, consider including the user identity in this log message, similar to what `CookieAuthenticationHandler` does. This would make it much easier to track token refresh events for specific users.

The phrasing 'signed in with refresh token' could also be more direct. 'Token refreshed' more accurately describes the event.

Consider changing this to:
```csharp
[LoggerMessage(11, LogLevel.Information, "AuthenticationScheme: {AuthenticationScheme} token refreshed for user: {UserName}.", EventName = "AuthenticationSchemeTokenRefreshed")]
public static partial void AuthenticationSchemeTokenRefreshed(this ILogger logger, string authenticationScheme, string userName);
```
A similar change to `AuthenticationSchemeSignedIn` to include the user name would also be beneficial for consistency.

Renaming `BearerTokenProtector` to `TokenProtector` is a sensible change. Since the handler now also deals with refresh tokens in addition to access tokens, this more generic name is more appropriate and less confusing.

Good call renaming `MessageReceived` to `MessageReceivedAsync`. This aligns with the .NET naming conventions for asynchronous methods and improves consistency with the newly added `SigningInAsync` method.
There's a small typo here: "written the to" should be "written to the".

The `RefreshToken` property is marked as `required`, but its XML comment ("If set...") and the OAuth 2.0 specification (RFC 6749, Section 5.1) both indicate that the refresh token is optional.

This `required` constraint seems incorrect and could cause runtime errors. For example, the related `SigningInContext.RefreshToken` property is nullable, and if it's null, creating an instance of `AccessTokenResponse` would fail.

To correctly model the optional nature of the refresh token, please consider changing this property to be nullable and removing the `required` modifier: `public string? RefreshToken { get; init; }`.

The dictionary-backed `RefreshToken` property was removed from here. This is a good change, as it's being replaced by a simple auto-property. This correctly treats the refresh token as a transient parameter for the sign-in operation, rather than a property to be persisted within the authentication ticket.

When re-adding the property, please consider improving its XML comment. The previous one (`If set, the token must be valid for sign in to continue.`) was a bit vague. Something like `Gets or sets the refresh token to be redeemed for a new access token.` would be clearer for developers.

The removal of the public API for `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` seems to contradict the 'Proposed API' section in the issue description, which shows this property being added. 

I suspect this is an intentional design change to use the `Items` dictionary instead, as discussed under 'Alternative Designs'. If that's the case, this change is correct, but it would be helpful to update the issue description to reflect the final design and avoid confusion.

Using `ArgumentException` here with `nameof(optionsMonitor)` as the parameter name seems a bit misleading. The `optionsMonitor` itself isn't the invalid argument; rather, it's the configuration state it represents that is invalid because `RefreshTokenProtector` is not set.

An `InvalidOperationException` would be more idiomatic for this kind of configuration error. For example:

```csharp
var refreshTokenProtector = identityBearerOptions.RefreshTokenProtector ?? 
    throw new InvalidOperationException("The RefreshTokenProtector must be configured on BearerTokenOptions to use the refresh endpoint.");
```

This makes it clearer that the application is in an invalid state due to missing configuration, rather than an incorrect argument being passed to this endpoint handler.

This is a good refactoring. Using dependency injection to provide the `TimeProvider` is a cleaner and more idiomatic approach in modern .NET compared to setting it directly on the options. This improves testability and aligns with best practices for using the `TimeProvider` abstraction.

This change removes the `OnSigningIn` event and `SigningInAsync` method. However, this event seems critical for the refresh token functionality described in the issue description, particularly for security stamp validation. The provided usage examples also rely on `OnSigningIn`. This removal appears to contradict the main goal of the PR. I see it's added back in a later commit; perhaps these commits could be squashed for a cleaner history?

This registration of `BearerTokenConfigureOptions` uses `IConfigureOptions<BearerTokenOptions>`, which will only apply the configuration to the default scheme (`Options.DefaultName`). This means if a developer registers a named bearer token scheme (e.g., `services.AddAuthentication().AddBearerToken("MyScheme")`), the default `TokenProtector` will not be configured for it. This would likely cause failures when trying to use refresh tokens with that scheme, as the `TokenProtector` would be null.

To ensure the default `TokenProtector` is available for all bearer token schemes, `BearerTokenConfigureOptions` should implement `IConfigureNamedOptions<BearerTokenOptions>` instead, and the service registration here should be for `IConfigureNamedOptions<BearerTokenOptions>`. This will ensure the configuration logic runs for any named instance of `BearerTokenOptions`.

The logic for handling refresh tokens appears to have been removed from `HandleSignInAsync`. The previous implementation correctly handled refresh by unprotecting the refresh token, extracting the principal, and then firing the `OnSigningIn` event before generating new tokens.

The new implementation no longer does this, and will instead generate new tokens for whatever `user` principal is passed in. In a refresh scenario, this would be a dummy principal, leading to invalid access tokens.

The PR description emphasizes the importance of the refresh flow and the `OnSigningIn` event. Was the refresh handling logic moved to an override of `SignInAsync` elsewhere in this file? Based on this diff alone, this change looks like it breaks token refreshing.

The exception message `"{nameof(BearerTokenProtector)} was not set."` could be confusing for developers. The XML documentation for this property states that a protector will be created if not provided, so it's not obvious why accessing this property would throw. This exception is really about accessing the property before it has been initialized (either by the user or by the framework's `PostConfigure` step).

To improve clarity, consider a more descriptive message, such as: `"Cannot access the '{nameof(BearerTokenProtector)}' until the options have been fully configured."`

This same feedback applies to the `RefreshTokenProtector` on line 61.

On a related note, the XML documentation for both properties could be slightly improved. Starting with "Gets or sets..." to describe what the property is would be more conventional than "If set...". For example: `/// Gets or sets the component used to protect and unprotect the bearer token.`

By removing `AuthenticationSchemeSignedInWithRefreshToken`, we lose the ability to distinguish between an initial login and a token refresh in the logs. This distinction can be very valuable for auditing and debugging purposes. Since the handler will have the context to know if it's performing a refresh (e.g., by checking for `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken`), could we consider keeping both log messages and using the appropriate one depending on the sign-in flow?

The new `RefreshTokenProtector` property, along with `BearerTokenProtector`, is a great improvement for API clarity over the previous generic `TokenProtector`.

Making these properties non-nullable is a strong design choice that improves security by ensuring tokens are always protected. To prevent this from causing runtime exceptions if a user doesn't configure them, can you confirm that default implementations for these protectors are registered (e.g., in a `PostConfigure` step)?

This call to `HandleException(error)` will cause a `StackOverflowException` for renderers that do not override `HandleException`, such as `WebAssemblyRenderer`.

The `WebAssemblyRenderer` uses the base `Renderer.HandleException` implementation, which calls `HandleExceptionViaErrorBoundary`. Your change here then calls `HandleException` again, creating an infinite recursion for any component running in an interactive WebAssembly mode.

While this pattern works for `WebRenderer` and `WebViewRenderer` (which override `HandleException` to throw), a robust solution must be safe for all renderer types.

The ideal fix might be to prevent `NavigationException` from being passed to the renderer's exception handler in the first place (e.g., by modifying `WebAssemblyDispatcher`). If the fix must be contained within the `Renderer` class, we need to find a way to bypass the error boundary logic for `NavigationException` without causing this recursion.

This is an excellent E2E test case. It perfectly reproduces the scenario described in the issue by wrapping a component that triggers a redirect within an ErrorBoundary. The assertions are thorough, verifying not only that the redirect to the correct URL occurs but also that the browser history is correctly maintained for 'back' navigation. This provides strong confidence in the fix.

This new test page is an excellent addition to validate the fix. It correctly sets up the scenario where a component that triggers a redirect (and thus a `NavigationException`) is placed within an `ErrorBoundary`. This will effectively test that `NavigationException` is no longer caught by the error boundary, allowing the redirect to complete as intended.

This new test case for an enhanced GET redirect within an error boundary is a great addition. The original issue description mentions login forms as a key scenario, which typically involves a POST request followed by a redirect. To ensure comprehensive coverage of the fix, would it be possible to also add a test case for a POST-based redirect occurring inside an error boundary?

This null check correctly fixes the `NullReferenceException` reported in the issue. To prevent this from regressing in the future, could you please add a unit test? A test that constructs `FormFeature` with an `IFormCollection` and then accesses the `Form` property would confirm the fix and protect against future changes.

The order of properties in the `required` array of a JSON Schema is not significant. This assertion relies on a specific order (`title`, then `completed`), which might be the current implementation detail (e.g., based on property declaration order) but could change in the future, making this test brittle.

To make the test more robust, I suggest asserting on the collection's contents without depending on the order. You could sort the list before the assertion.

For example:
```csharp
var requiredProperties = schema.Required.OrderBy(p => p).ToList();
Assert.Collection(requiredProperties,
    property => Assert.Equal("completed", property),
    property => Assert.Equal("title", property));
```

The `schema.Required` property is an `ISet<string>`, which doesn't guarantee a specific enumeration order. Using `Assert.Collection` here could lead to flaky tests because the order of properties in the set is not guaranteed.

To make this assertion more robust, I'd suggest either sorting the collection before asserting or using order-independent assertions.

For example, you could sort it:
```csharp
Assert.Collection(schema.Required.OrderBy(p => p),
    property => Assert.Equal("completed", property),
    property => Assert.Equal("title", property));
```

Alternatively, you could check for presence without asserting order:
```csharp
Assert.Equal(2, schema.Required.Count);
Assert.Contains("title", schema.Required);
Assert.Contains("completed", schema.Required);
```

Using `[Required]` on a non-nullable value type like `bool` is a good test case. It correctly tests that the schema generation logic considers the attribute as a signal for property presence, even though it has no effect on runtime validation for non-nullable types.

To make the test coverage for this new feature even more comprehensive, consider also adding a `[Required]` nullable value type property, for example: `[Required] public int? Count { get; set; }`. This would cover another very common use case.

This test case for a `float` parameter with a `DefaultValueAttribute` seems to be asserting incorrect behavior. It expects the default value to be an `OpenApiInteger`, but for a `float` type, it should be an `OpenApiFloat` (or `OpenApiDouble`) to correctly represent a `number` in the OpenAPI specification.

The assertion should probably be:
```csharp
[([DefaultValue(3f)] float id) => { }, (IOpenApiAny defaultValue) => Assert.Equal(3f, ((OpenApiFloat)defaultValue).Value)],
```
This change will likely cause the test to fail, which would indicate that the implementation for handling `DefaultValueAttribute` on `float` types is not correct and needs to be fixed. The current test validates that a `float` default is incorrectly treated as an integer.

This test correctly verifies that `[DefaultValue(true)]` is reflected in the generated schema for a boolean property.

To make this integration test more comprehensive, consider also testing `[DefaultValue]` with other data types. You could add `[DefaultValue]` attributes to the `Id` (integer) and `Name` (string) properties of the `ProjectBoard` class and then add assertions for their default values in the schema here. This would provide stronger validation that `DefaultValueAttribute` is handled correctly for various types through the entire request body schema generation pipeline.

For example, in `ProjectBoard`:
```csharp
[DefaultValue(1)]
public int Id { get; set; }

[DefaultValue("My board")]
public string Name { get; set; }
```
And then update the assertions for `id` and `name` properties in this test and in `GetOpenApiResponse_GeneratesSchemaForPoco_WithValidationAttributes`.

The test name focuses on 'ValidationAttributes', but a key part of this test, and the main goal of this PR, is to verify support for `DefaultValueAttribute`. Consider renaming the test to be more explicit about this, for example, `...WithDefaultValueAndValidationAttributes` to better reflect its full scope and improve discoverability.

The `required` keyword makes `IsPrivate` a required property. However, the `[DefaultValue]` attribute is typically used to specify a default for an *optional* property.

Combining `required` with `[DefaultValue]` can lead to a confusing OpenAPI schema. A property that is marked as required must be provided by the client, so a default value for when it's absent is contradictory. While the OpenAPI specification doesn't strictly forbid this, it can be confusing for API consumers and may be flagged as an issue by some OpenAPI tools.

To provide a clearer and more common test case for the `[DefaultValue]` attribute's functionality, I suggest making this property optional by removing the `required` keyword:

```csharp
[DefaultValue(true)]
public bool IsPrivate { get; set; }
```

This change would better reflect the typical use case for `[DefaultValue]` and result in a more idiomatic OpenAPI schema.

This change significantly simplifies the `parse` method by delegating the responsibility of identifying Starlark-like options to the `nativeOptionsParser`. Instead of this class manually filtering the residue for arguments starting with `--`, it now operates on a pre-filtered list from `getSkippedArgs()`.

This is a great refactoring. It improves the separation of concerns between the native options parser and the Starlark options parser. Assuming `getSkippedArgs()` now correctly provides all Starlark option arguments in the order they were encountered (including from `.bazelrc` expansions), this should correctly fix the flag override issue described in #13231.

This is a key part of the fix. By checking `getSkippedArgs()` here, we ensure that if residue is disallowed, we correctly fail when Starlark flags are present (as they are now treated as 'skipped args' instead of residue).

The updated error message on the following lines, which concatenates both lists, is also a great improvement for user clarity. This change correctly reflects that 'skipped args' are effectively a form of residue from the perspective of the core options parser.

This is a clever solution to the ordering problem. By treating skipped arguments (like Starlark flags) as values of a synthetic `allowMultiple` option, you're able to leverage the existing parser's ordering and expansion logic correctly.

A key consequence of this change is that these arguments will no longer appear in the parser's residue (`getResidue()`). The PR description mentions this, but it's a significant change to the parser's contract. Have you verified that all consumers of `getResidue()` have been updated to use the new `getSkippedArgs()` method where necessary? This seems to be the case in other parts of the PR, but it's a critical point to double-check to avoid breaking other commands.

This test asserts that Starlark flags are rejected when `allowResidue` is false. Is this the intended behavior?

The pull request description mentions that Starlark flags are now handled via a synthetic `allowMultiple` option and are no longer considered part of the residue. If they are treated as options, it seems they should be parsable even when `allowResidue` is `false`, similar to other options.

If this behavior is intentional, could you add a comment explaining why Starlark flags are disallowed when residue is not allowed? Otherwise, this test might be revealing an unintended side effect where "skipped args" are still being handled by the residue logic.

To make this test more robust and fully verify the new behavior, could you please change `contains` to `containsExactly`? This ensures no other unexpected arguments are present in `getSkippedArgs()` and makes this test consistent with others in this file that use `isEqualTo`.

Additionally, since the PR description states that "the residue... now only contain[s] build targets," it would be great to add an assertion to confirm that the residue is empty in this test case:

```java
assertThat(parser.getResidue()).isEmpty();
```

This change in behavior, from treating single-dashed Starlark-like flags (`-//...`) as residue to throwing an `OptionsParsingException`, is a significant improvement. It makes the parsing behavior for Starlark flags more consistent with standard flag parsing and provides clear, immediate feedback to the user on invalid syntax. This prevents subtle bugs where a user might think they are setting a flag, but it's being silently ignored.

This update to use `parseGivenArgs` is a good simplification, as it removes the need for the test-only `setResidueForTesting` method.

A minor note on the argument parsing (which also applied to the previous code): `options.split(" ")` is fragile and won't handle flag values with spaces.

A more robust approach would be to change the method signature to take `String... options`. This would align it with similar helpers like `parseStarlarkOptions` in `StarlarkOptionsParserTest`. However, since this is a `protected` method in a test base class, changing the signature might be a breaking change for other tests. If so, this can be addressed separately. It's not a blocker for this PR.

This new `py_test` is a great addition to verify the fix for overriding Starlark flags. The test name is clear and its structure is consistent with other tests in this file. This regression test is crucial for ensuring the issue with platform-specific configs and command-line flag precedence doesn't reappear.

This test correctly verifies the behavior for explicit `--config` flags. However, the original bug report (#13231) was about an *implicitly* applied platform-specific config (`build:linux`), which couldn't be overridden. The behavior for implicit configs was different from explicit ones before this change.

To make the test suite more comprehensive and ensure the original bug is fully covered, could you please add another test case for an implicit config? You could use a command-specific config to avoid platform dependency, for example:

`.bazelrc`:
`build --//f:color=red`

Command:
`bazel build --//f:color=green ...`

Here, `green` should be the final value. This would more closely mirror the scenario described in the issue.

The hardcoded line and column number (`9:10`) in this regular expression make the test brittle. Minor formatting changes in `f/f.bzl` could easily break this assertion.

To make the test more robust, please consider using `\d+` to match the line and column numbers. For example:
`re.match(r"DEBUG: .*/f/f.bzl:\d+:\d+: red", line)`

This change would also apply to the similar assertion on line 65.

The phrasing "Exactly one of ... can be set to ..." is ambiguous. It could be misinterpreted as requiring that one of the parameters *must* be set to the value `'all'`. 

Based on the design document, the intent is that `'all'` can be used for at most one of the parameters. A clearer phrasing would be:
"At most one of `root_module_direct_deps` and `root_module_direct_dev_deps` can be set to the special value `'all'`."

This also applies to the identical text for `root_module_direct_dev_deps` on line 167.

The `:bzlmod` library contains core data structures for Bazel modules (e.g., `Module`, `ModuleKey`), while `:common` contains helper classes for repository rule creation. Adding a dependency from `:bzlmod` to `:common` seems to invert the typical dependency direction, where implementation/helper code depends on core data structures, not the other way around. This can make the core data model less reusable and harder to understand in isolation.

Could you elaborate on why this new dependency is necessary? It might be worth considering if some classes could be moved between targets to maintain a clearer dependency graph and avoid this dependency inversion.

Using `LinkedHashSet` as the intermediate representation for serialization is a good choice. It preserves the iteration order of the `ImmutableSet`, which is crucial for deterministic output in files like `bazel-lock.json`. This helps prevent spurious diffs.

This is correct, but for better readability and maintainability, it would be great to keep this block of `registerTypeAdapterFactory` calls for immutable collections sorted alphabetically. The same applies to the corresponding static imports on lines 18-21.

The documentation for this method and its parameters (lines 130 and 139) are placeholders (`"foo"`). Please update them to accurately describe the functionality, as outlined in the design document.

For example, the method docstring should explain that this function specifies which repositories generated by the extension should be automatically brought into scope and that it should be returned from the extension's implementation function. The parameter docs should also be filled out based on the design doc.

The buildozer target `//MODULE.bazel:all` seems incorrect. This target refers to the `all` target in a package named `MODULE.bazel`, which would require a `MODULE.bazel/` directory with a `BUILD` file at the workspace root.

The intended target is likely the `MODULE.bazel` file at the workspace root. Bazel implicitly creates a `filegroup` target for `MODULE.bazel` files. For the root `MODULE.bazel` file, this target is `//:MODULE.bazel`. The proposal document for this feature also refers to `//:MODULE.bazel` as the typical target.

Please consider changing `//MODULE.bazel:all` to `//:MODULE.bazel` to correctly target the root module file.

The Javadoc for this method is a bit confusing. The term "proxy" likely refers to the `ModuleExtensionProxy` object, which isn't itself "marked as a dev dependency". It's the `bazel_dep` on the module that *defines* the extension that can have `dev_dependency = True`.

To improve clarity, how about rephrasing this to something like:

"The repo names imported from this extension usage that are considered to be from a dev dependency. This is the case when the `bazel_dep` on the module defining the extension has `dev_dependency = True`."

This implementation for deriving the `ModuleKey` from the location seems to have a couple of issues and is quite fragile:

1. For external modules, the location file path is typically of the form `external/<canonical-repo-name>/MODULE.bazel`. The current substring logic doesn't account for the `external/` prefix.
2. `ModuleKey.fromString` expects a `name@version` format, but the canonical repo name in the path is in `name~version` format.

This will lead to an `IllegalStateException` at runtime. A more robust implementation would need to handle the `external/` prefix and parse the `name~version` string (perhaps by splitting on the last `~`). It might be cleaner to introduce a new static factory method on `ModuleKey`, like `fromCanonicalRepoName`, to encapsulate this parsing logic.

This correctly records the `exportedName` of a repository as a development dependency. Using `exportedName` is the right choice, as it's the canonical identifier for the repository from the extension, unlike `localRepoName` which is a local alias. This correctly captures the information needed for automatic `use_repo` fixups.

While module names are not supposed to contain the `@` character, this is not enforced when creating a `ModuleKey`. If a `ModuleKey` is created with a name containing `@` (e.g., `ModuleKey.create("a@b", "1.0")`), its `toString()` representation would be `a@b@1.0`. This `fromString` method would then incorrectly parse it, taking `a` as the name and `b@1.0` as the version.

Using `lastIndexOf('@')` would make this method more robust by correctly parsing the rightmost part as the version.

Instead of passing just the collection of usages (`.values()`), consider passing the entire `usagesValue.getExtensionUsages()` map. This would give the `evaluate` method more context, such as which module corresponds to which usage, and allow for efficient lookup (e.g., for the root module's usage via `get(ModuleKey.ROOT)`) instead of iterating over the collection.

This would require updating the signature of `ModuleExtensionMetadata.evaluate` to accept a `Map<ModuleKey, ModuleExtensionUsage>`.

Adding a dependency to a shared test library like `bzlmod_tests_lib` can affect all tests that depend on it. If the new test logic requiring the `events` package is self-contained, consider creating a new, more specific `java_library` for those tests. This would help keep the dependency set of this common library lean and avoid potentially increasing the overhead for other tests.

The buildozer target `//MODULE.bazel:all` seems incorrect. For `use_repo` commands, buildozer expects a target that is a label pointing to the `MODULE.bazel` file itself.

According to buildozer's documentation for `use_repo_*` commands, "The target must be of the form `//path/to:MODULE.bazel`". For a `MODULE.bazel` file at the workspace root (as indicated by the `Location` in the test setup), this translates to `//:MODULE.bazel`.

Please update this test assertion and the corresponding message generation logic to use the correct target format. This issue appears in all generated buildozer commands in this file.

This is a good update to the test helper. Explicitly setting `devImports` to an empty set here is a clean way to handle the new field. It ensures that tests using this base builder have a sensible default and continue to work as expected, without needing to be aware of `devImports` unless they are specifically testing that functionality.

The phrasing here, "the `cc_library` target", could be a bit confusing as it's singular, while the check is performed for each instance of the rule. A few lines down (line 248), the same behavior (when the flag is disabled) is described as being "visible to every instance of the rule".

To improve clarity and consistency, could we rephrase this line? For example:

`respect to each instance of the rule (the `cc_library` target in our example).`

This would make it clearer that the check applies to all instances and aligns the terminology used throughout this section.

The logic here is correct and implements the intended fallback mechanism. The accompanying comments are also very helpful in explaining the "why".

For slightly improved readability, consider reframing the condition using De Morgan's laws. The current logic is `!visibleFromDefinition && !visibleFromUsage`. This could be expressed as `!(visibleFromDefinition || visibleFromUsage)`, which might more directly communicate the intent: "error if the prerequisite is visible from neither the rule's definition nor its usage context".

```suggestion
          && !(RuleContext.isVisible(implicitDefinition, prerequisite.getConfiguredTarget())
              || context.isVisible(prerequisite.getConfiguredTarget()))) {
```

This is a minor stylistic suggestion, and the current implementation is perfectly fine functionally.

This documentation update is crucial for explaining the new fallback behavior. The change from "rather than" to describing a fallback is a great improvement.

For even greater clarity, you might consider making the condition for the fallback more explicit. For example:

`...to the rule definition. If a dependency is not visible from the definition, visibility is checked from the rule's usage location as a fallback.`

However, I understand the need for conciseness in help texts, so the current version is also acceptable if you prefer brevity.

Instead of making `stats` nullable, could we make it non-nullable and pass a no-op implementation of `RemoteStats` when stats collection is not needed? This would avoid null checks in `RemoteSpawnRunner` and make the code cleaner. This design (using the Null Object Pattern) is generally preferred over passing nulls.
Nit: please add a space after `if` to match the coding style used elsewhere in this file and the Google Java Style Guide.

Java convention for field names is camelCase (e.g., `totalSpawns`, `remoteCacheHit`). It would be good to apply this convention to all the fields in this class for consistency with standard Java style.
This trailing semicolon is unnecessary and inconsistent with the other methods in this class. It should be removed.

While passing `null` is valid here since the new dependency is `@Nullable`, consider using a mock object like `Mockito.mock(RemoteActionStats.class)`. This would make the test's intent more explicit and would allow for verifications (e.g., `verify(mock, never()).someMethod()`) to ensure that statistics are not modified in tests where they shouldn't be. This is a minor style point that can improve test clarity and robustness.

While passing `null` for the new dependency (presumably for action stats) gets the existing tests to compile, it's a bit fragile as it relies on the `RemoteSpawnRunner` being null-safe. 

A more robust and standard approach in unit tests is to pass a mock object, for example, `mock(RemoteActionStats.class)`. This avoids potential `NullPointerException`s and makes the test's intent clearer (i.e., that this dependency is not under test here).

Could you please update this and the other instantiations in this test file to use a mock instead of `null`?

This function has the same name as the one defined above (starting on line 513). In shell scripts, this will overwrite the previous function definition, meaning only this second test will be run. 

Please rename this function to something more descriptive of the scenario it tests, for example, `test_remote_stats_with_fallback`.

This new helper function nicely encapsulates the logic for propagating tags to actions, which cleans up the code here significantly.

Could you clarify the merging strategy implemented in `getFilteredExecutionInfo`? Specifically, what happens if there's a conflict between a tag on the target (e.g., `no-remote`) and an `execution_requirement` on the action (e.g., `remote`)? The original issue raised this question, and it would be helpful to understand the precedence rules, perhaps by documenting them in a Javadoc for the new method.

For checking membership against a fixed set of strings, using an `ImmutableSet` and its `contains` method is generally more idiomatic and can be more performant than a chain of `||` with `equals`.

Consider replacing the predicate with a static `ImmutableSet<String>`:

```java
private static final ImmutableSet<String> PROPAGATED_TAGS =
    ImmutableSet.of(
        "no-remote",
        "no-cache",
        "no-sandbox",
        "no-remote-exec",
        "no-remote-cache");
```

Then, in `getExecutionInfoFromTags`, you can use `if (PROPAGATED_TAGS.contains(tag))`. This would also make the list of tags easier to read and maintain.

The test name `testExecutionInfoCopied` is a bit misleading. This test actually verifies that execution requirements are *inherited* from the action owner's tags, not just copied, as it passes an empty map for execution info and asserts that a tag from the owner (`local`) is present in the resulting action's execution info.

A more descriptive name like `testExecutionInfoInheritsTagsFromOwner` would better reflect the behavior under test.

Additionally, this test appears to rely on a shared `actionConstructionContext` fixture being set up with an `ActionOwner` that has the `local` tag. This implicit dependency makes the test harder to understand in isolation. Consider making the test self-contained by creating a specific `ActionOwner` and `ActionConstructionContext` within this test method to make the test's setup and intent explicit.

This test case appears to use a typo: `supports-worker` (singular) instead of the standard `supports-workers` (plural).

If this test passes as is, it suggests that the implementation of `getFilteredExecutionInfo` might be incorrectly treating `supports-worker` as a valid execution requirement. The other test case in this method correctly filters out `some-custom-tag`, so it's surprising that `supports-worker` would be allowed.

Could you please change this to `supports-workers`? It would also be beneficial to add a test case that explicitly asserts that an invalid key (like the typo `supports-worker`) is filtered out, to ensure the filtering logic is robust.

Using `Runtime.NONE` here as the `uncheckedExecRequirements` argument is confusing. This parameter is expected to be a `SkylarkDict` (representing `exec_properties`), or `null` if there are none. `Runtime.NONE` is a `StarlarkSemantics` object.

While the implementation might be robust enough to handle this by ignoring non-Map objects, using `null` would be much clearer to indicate that there are no Starlark-defined execution requirements in this test case, consistent with the test on line 119.

Minor nit: For consistency with the language's current name, could you please rename this test and its corresponding `.sh` file to use `starlark` instead of `skylark`? E.g., `tags_propagation_starlark_test`.

This function name appears to contain a typo. The suffix `_шт_кгду` corresponds to `_in_rule` when typed on a QWERTY keyboard with a Russian layout. For clarity and maintainability, please correct the function name, for example to `test_tags_propagated_to_run_with_exec_info_in_rule`.

Is there a specific reason this example is disabled for wasm? The `flate2` crate seems to have wasm support. It would be great to have this example working on wasm as well if it's not too much trouble to get it working, as it demonstrates a powerful asset pipeline feature.

This new binary asset is for the `asset_decompression` example. Could you please clarify its origin and license? All assets in the Bevy repository must have a license compatible with Bevy's (MIT/Apache-2.0). If you created this image, you can state that it's licensed under the same terms. If it's from a third party, we need to ensure its license is compatible and provide attribution if required.

This logic is correct. For improved clarity and consistency with `maybe_layers`, consider renaming the `volumetric` variable in the closure arguments on lines 99 and 117 to `maybe_volumetric`. This would make it more explicit that an `Option` is being checked here to produce the boolean `volumetric` field.

With the addition of `is_volumetric_light`, this function signature is becoming quite long and the tuple destructuring is a bit verbose. 

Have you considered using a small struct for the parameters to improve readability? For example:

```rust
struct LightSortKey<'a> {
    entity: &'a Entity,
    shadows_enabled: &'a bool,
    is_volumetric: &'a bool,
    is_spot_light: &'a bool,
}

pub(crate) fn clusterable_object_order(light1: LightSortKey, light2: LightSortKey) -> std::cmp::Ordering {
    light1.is_spot_light.cmp(light2.is_spot_light) // pointlights before spot lights
        .then_with(|| light2.shadows_enabled.cmp(light1.shadows_enabled)) // shadow casters before non-casters
        .then_with(|| light2.is_volumetric.cmp(light1.is_volumetric)) // volumetric lights before non-volumetric lights
        .then_with(|| light1.entity.cmp(light2.entity)) // stable
}
```

This is just a suggestion for a potential cleanup and not a blocker for this PR, as the current implementation is consistent with the existing code style.

The variable `point_light_volumetric_enabled_count` seems misnamed given this condition. The logic `light.volumetric || light.shadows_enabled` suggests it's counting all point lights that use a cubemap (for either volumetric fog or shadows), not just volumetric ones. This is likely correct if they share the same resource pool, but the name is confusing. A more accurate name would be `point_light_cubemap_user_count` or similar.

More importantly, this seems related to a potential bug on lines 738-746, where the `VOLUMETRIC` flag is only set if `light.shadows_enabled && light.volumetric`. This prevents non-shadow-casting lights from contributing to volumetric fog, which seems to contradict the goal of this PR. 

If it's not a technical requirement for volumetric lights to also cast shadows, the condition to set the flag should likely be changed, and this counting logic might need to be revisited as well.
The variable `P_view` is calculated here but doesn't appear to be used anywhere within its scope in the loop. It seems to be dead code and can be removed.

The use of `bounding_radius` here seems incorrect for point and spot lights. `bounding_radius` is a global property related to the fog volume for directional lights, and doesn't represent the distance light travels from a point/spot light source to a sample point in the fog.

For a more physically correct attenuation through the medium, you should use the distance from the light to the current sample point `P_world`. You've already calculated `distance_square` on line 340, so you can use that.

Consider replacing this line with something like:
```wgsl
let distance_to_light = sqrt(distance_square);
let light_attenuation = exp(-distance_to_light * density * (absorption + scattering));
```
This would correctly model the light extinction through the participating medium for point and spot lights.

These hardcoded transform values make it difficult to understand the spotlight's position and orientation. The separate steps to create and then rotate the transform are also a bit verbose.

For better readability in this example, consider using `Transform::looking_at` to define the orientation if the spotlight is aimed at a particular point. For example:

```rust
let spotlight_transform = Transform::from_xyz(x, y, z).looking_at(target, Vec3::Y);
```

If `looking_at` isn't suitable, you could construct the transform more concisely in one go:

```rust
let spotlight_transform = Transform::from_xyz(-1.78, 3.90, -2.71)
    .with_rotation(Quat::from_xyzw(-0.83, 0.10, -0.17, 0.51));
```

Rounding the values slightly could also help readability if exact precision isn't critical for the example's visual effect.

Using a dedicated module (`fq_std`) to provide fully-qualified paths for standard library types is an excellent strategy for improving proc-macro hygiene. This change effectively prevents name collisions with user-defined types, directly addressing the reported issue. This is a great pattern to enforce across all our proc-macros.

This is a good change for consistency. While the previous code was already hygienic for `Option` by using a fully qualified path, this new approach with `FQOption` centralizes the path definitions, which improves maintainability. It also makes the code in this function a bit cleaner by removing the temporary `option` variable.

While the original code using `::core::default::Default::default()` was already hygienic, this change to use `#FQDefault` is good for consistency. Centralizing the fully qualified paths in `fq_std.rs`, as is being done in this pull request, should make the proc-macros more maintainable in the long run.

For consistency with the other fully-qualified paths that use `::core`, and to better support `no_std` environments, it would be better to use `::alloc::boxed::Box` here. `Box` lives in the `alloc` crate, and `std` just re-exports it. Using `::alloc` would be more fundamental and align with the use of `::core` for the other types.
